title: 21x Hackathon Winner Turned College Dropout | Jia Chen Interview
guest_role: 21x hackathon winner, college dropout, co-founder of Sprint.dev, content creator

sections.title: Standing Out From a Non-Target School

key_takeaways: Winning hackathons comes down to team dynamics, judge interaction, and smart project execution, not stacking credentials or showing off a complicated tech stack.
Pick hackathon teammates on skills and drive rather than credentials: past projects, GitHub activity, and ambition beat resumes; Jia has won with determined community-college students.
Keep hackathon presentations short and let judges interact with the project; teams that spend the time talking about their tech stack often lose.
Splitting into separate frontend and backend teams and merging late usually fails at hackathons; connect both early and iterate together.
Hackathons double as recruiting pipelines: recruiters at larger hackathons scout future interns and fast-track candidates for internship applications.
From a non-target school, make yourself the outlier: take leadership roles, spearhead big projects, and build a personal brand.
Develop 'agency' - the ability to take action and think on your feet - plus a 'get it done and move on' time-efficiency mindset.
College provides structure if you don't know what to learn but isn't necessary; for Jia it eventually dulled her creativity and passion for learning.
Don't drop out on a whim: set preconditions first by building projects, testing the idea, and evaluating your technical skills and investor network.
As a founder, move fast and continuously ship while avoiding burnout, and stay open to connections and learning opportunities that don't seem immediately useful.
